explanation:
**Northern Hemisphere gardening** combines agronomy, botany, and regional climate patterns. The USDA and regional extension services provide zone-specific growing guides.

## How It Works

Select your month to see recommended crops for your region. This calculator covers Mexico, the USA, and Spain—three zones with distinct climate patterns and growing seasons.

The guide shows typical planting windows for the Northern Hemisphere, checking seasonal ranges and noting how climate varies significantly across regions.

## Important Notes

This is a reference guide for general information. For critical agricultural or business decisions, consult your local extension office or agricultural professionals. Information is current through 2026 and updated regularly.

faq.q: What should I plant in January in the Northern Hemisphere?
faq.a: January is mid-winter in the North. Outdoor planting is limited. Focus on cold-hardy crops: garlic, winter lettuce, spinach. Start seeds indoors for spring transplants.

faq.q: What's a USDA hardiness zone and why does it matter?
faq.a: USDA zones (1-13) show your average coldest winter temperatures. They determine frost dates and what perennials survive. Find yours at planthardiness.ars.usda.gov.

faq.q: Should I use a greenhouse or plant outdoors?
faq.a: Greenhouses extend seasons by 4-6 weeks on both ends. Start seeds indoors there, then transplant outside after frost. Outdoor planting is free but frost-limited.

faq.q: What's the difference between first frost and last frost date?
faq.a: Last frost date (spring) = your last freeze before warm season. First frost date (fall) = your first freeze after summer. Both define your outdoor planting window.

faq.q: How often should I plant seeds for continuous harvest?
faq.a: Plant every 2-3 weeks for succession planting. This staggers your harvests so you get fresh crops all season instead of one big flush.
Leafy greens (lettuce, spinach, kale), herbs (parsley, mint), and root crops (beets, carrots) tolerate 3-6 hours of sun. Most fruiting crops need 6-8+ hours.

faq.q: Is the planting calendar accurate for my exact location?
faq.a: This is a reference for the Northern Hemisphere. Local climate varies significantly by zone and elevation. Check your USDA zone or regional extension office for exact frost dates.
Yes. All Hacé Cuentas calculators are free and require no registration.
